remedy: Rake up and destroy fallen leaves to reduce overwintering spores. Apply fungicides like Captan, Myclobutanil, or Mancozeb at the green tip stage. Prune trees to improve air circulation.
Prune out dead wood and remove mummified fruit from the tree. Treat with fungicides such as Thiophanate-methyl or Captan. Ensure proper sanitation in the orchard.
Remove nearby juniper (red cedar) hosts if possible. Apply fungicides like Myclobutanil or Fenarimol starting at the pink stage of bud development.
Prune to improve air circulation and light penetration. Apply sulfur, potassium bicarbonate, or synthetic fungicides like Myclobutanil at the first sign of disease.
Plant resistant corn hybrids. Apply fungicides (e.g., Azoxystrobin + Propiconazole) if infection is severe and occurs early in the season.
Rotate crops to reduce inoculum. Use resistant varieties. Apply fungicides like Mancozeb or Pyraclostrobin if lesions appear before silking.
Remove mummified berries and infected canes. Apply fungicides (Mancozeb, Myclobutanil) from bud break through 4 weeks post-bloom.
There is no cure. Remove and destroy infected vines to prevent spread. Protect pruning wounds with sealants or fungicides.
Plant resistant varieties (e.g., Candor, Southern Pearl). Apply copper sprays at leaf drop in fall and before bud swell in spring.
Use disease-free seeds and transplants. Rotate crops every 2-3 years. Apply copper-based bactericides mixed with Mancozeb.
Maintain plant vigor with proper nitrogen and irrigation. Apply fungicides like Chlorothalonil, Mancozeb, or Azoxystrobin on a schedule.
Destroy all infected tubers and cull piles. Apply fungicides like Metalaxyl, Cymoxanil, or Chlorothalonil preventatively. Monitor weather conditions.
Use resistant varieties. Improve soil fertility (especially Potassium and Silicon). Treat seeds with fungicides like Iprodione or Carbendazim.
Avoid excessive nitrogen application. Maintain consistent flood depth. Apply systemic fungicides like Tricyclazole or Isoprothiolane.
Use resistant varieties (e.g., IR64). Avoid nitrogen fertilizer during severe outbreaks. Drain the field to reduce humidity.
Control green leafhopper vectors using insecticides (Imidacloprid). Plant resistant varieties. Practice synchronous planting.
Remove infected plants. Avoid overhead watering to reduce splash spread. Apply copper sprays mixed with Mancozeb.
Mulch soil to prevent spore splash. Stake plants for better airflow. Apply fungicides like Chlorothalonil or Copper-based products.
Remove and destroy infected plant parts immediately. Apply copper-based fungicides or Chlorothalonil on a 7-10 day schedule.
Increase air circulation by spacing and pruning. Reduce greenhouse humidity. Apply fungicides like Chlorothalonil or Mancozeb.
Remove lower leaves to improve airflow. Mulch the base of plants. Apply fungicides like Chlorothalonil or Copper soap.
Remove and destroy infected plants. Wash hands thoroughly after using tobacco products. Control aphids which can transmit viruses.
Plant resistant varieties. Apply fungicides like Tebuconazole, Propiconazole, or Azoxystrobin at the first sign of infection.
Use resistant cultivars. Apply foliar fungicides (Triazoles or Strobilurins) when the flag leaf is emerging.
Use certified disease-free seed. Treat seeds with systemic fungicides like Carboxin or Tebuconazole before planting.
Use acid-delinted seeds. Plant resistant varieties. Destroy crop residues after harvest to reduce overwintering bacteria.
Control whitefly vectors using insecticides (e.g., Diafenthiuron). Use resistant varieties. Remove weed hosts from the field.
Use healthy setts for planting. Follow a crop rotation of 2-3 years. Treat setts with Carbendazim before planting.
Remove and burn smut whips (whip-like structures). Use resistant varieties. Treat setts with hot water (50°C for 2 hours) before planting.
Monitor sentinel plots. Apply fungicides (Triazoles/Strobilurins) preventatively or at the very first sign of disease.
Prune infected twigs and branches. Spray with copper-based bactericides to protect new growth. Use windbreaks to reduce spread.
Remove and destroy infected trees immediately. Control the Asian Citrus Psyllid vector with insecticides. Use disease-free nursery stock.
Control aphid vectors. Remove weed hosts. Use resistant varieties. Disinfect tools to prevent mechanical transmission.
Apply sulfur or potassium bicarbonate sprays. Plant resistant varieties. Improve air circulation by spacing vines.
Remove and destroy infected fruits. Stake plants to keep fruit off the ground. Apply fungicides like Copper oxychloride or Mancozeb.
Remove infected leaves and prune canes. Avoid overhead watering. Apply fungicides containing Chlorothalonil or Propiconazole.
Use disease-free seed. Rotate crops for at least 2 years. Apply fungicides like Chlorothalonil if weather is cool and wet.
Remove decaying fruit and leaves. Handle fruit gently. Apply fungicides during bloom to protect flowers.
Plant rows in the direction of prevailing winds for airflow. Apply fungicides like Mancozeb or Chlorothalonil preventatively.
Control whitefly vectors. Roguing of infected plants. Use virus-resistant hybrids.
Spray Carbendazim or Mancozeb. Use resistant varieties. Clean cultivation to remove plant debris.
Prune affected parts and burn them. Spray Streptocycline + Copper Oxychloride. Maintain orchard sanitation.
The plant appears healthy. Continue with regular irrigation, fertilization, and pest monitoring practices.
